questions.question: Explain the purpose of SCM_RIGHTS in Unix file descriptor passing.
keyPoints: SCM_RIGHTS is a control message type used to transfer file descriptors
It is passed as ancillary data in sendmsg()/recvmsg()
Allows one process to give another access to an already-open file without reopening

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in sending a file descriptor from process A to process B.
keyPoints: Create Unix domain socket pair or connection
Prepare msghdr structure with control message buffer
Fill cmsghdr: cmsg_level=SOL_SOCKET, cmsg_type=SCM_RIGHTS
Place file descriptor value in cmsg_data area
Use sendmsg() to transmit and recvmsg() to receive
Both processes must close their respective FDs when done

questions.question: What is FD_CLOEXEC and why is it important in file descriptor passing?
keyPoints: FD_CLOEXEC is a flag that marks a file descriptor for close-on-exec
When set, the FD is automatically closed when the process calls exec()
Prevents file descriptors from being leaked to executed programs
Important in fork+exec patterns when passing FDs to child processes

questions.question: Explain the relationship between file descriptors and open file descriptions in the kernel.
keyPoints: File descriptors are per-process integer indices into the FD table
Open file descriptions are kernel structures representing open files
Multiple FDs in same or different processes can point to same open file description
Changes like file offset are visible across all references to that open file description

questions.question: What are Unix domain sockets and why are they required for passing file descriptors?
keyPoints: Unix domain sockets (AF_UNIX) are for local IPC between processes on the same host
They provide reliable, ordered communication without network overhead
They support ancillary data mechanism (control messages) needed for SCM_RIGHTS
Other socket types may not reliably support passing file descriptors

questions.question: Explain the cmsghdr structure and its fields used for file descriptor passing.
keyPoints: cmsghdr contains control message metadata
cmsg_len: total length of control message including data
cmsg_level: protocol level (SOL_SOCKET for SCM_RIGHTS)
cmsg_type: message type (SCM_RIGHTS for file descriptors)
cmsg_data: contains the actual file descriptor integer(s)

questions.question: Describe a practical use case of passing file descriptors in Unix systems.
keyPoints: Privilege separation in servers: parent accepts connections, passes to unprivileged child
Implementing custom IPC mechanisms beyond pipes/sockets
Delegating access to files without giving file system path
In systemd socket activation: systemd passes accepted sockets to daemon

questions.question: Write a C program demonstrating passing a file descriptor from a parent process to a child process using Unix domain sockets and SCM_RIGHTS.
keyPoints: Create socket pair using socketpair() with AF_UNIX
Fork to create child process
Parent: prepare msghdr, allocate cmsghdr, set SCM_RIGHTS, sendmsg() with FD
Child: use recvmsg() to receive FD through cmsghdr
Both processes verify received FD, perform I/O operations
Close file descriptors appropriately in both processes
Error handling and cleanup

questions.question: Explain the complete mechanism of file descriptor passing in Unix/Linux, including the data structures involved and system calls used.
keyPoints: Unix domain socket creation (socket() or socketpair())
msghdr structure setup with msg_control and msg_controllen
cmsghdr structure: cmsg_level=SOL_SOCKET, cmsg_type=SCM_RIGHTS
CMSG_FIRSTHDR and CMSG_DATA macros for accessing control data
sendmsg() on sender side with ancillary data containing FD
recvmsg() on receiver side extracting FD from control message
Kernel increments file reference count
Both processes have independent FDs pointing to same open file description
Proper cleanup: both must close their FDs

questions.question: Discuss the security implications and advantages of using file descriptor passing for inter-process communication.
keyPoints: Advantages: No need to reopen files, preserves file position, efficient, enables privilege delegation
Security: Parent can pass specific FDs to child without exposing file system paths
Privilege separation: Run services with minimal privileges, delegate only needed resources
Avoids race conditions of opening files by path
Prevents unauthorized access to files outside intended scope
Challenges: Both processes must coordinate, need Unix domain sockets, proper error handling required
FD_CLOEXEC prevents leakage to untrusted executed programs

questions.question: Compare file descriptor passing with traditional IPC mechanisms (pipes, message queues) for file sharing. When would you choose each approach?
keyPoints: File descriptor passing: Direct sharing of open file handles, preserves file position, supports any file type, requires Unix domain sockets
Pipes: Unidirectional byte stream, simple but limited to pipe/socket/FIFO, no shared file position
Message queues: Kernel-persisted, structured messages, but not for file sharing
Choose FD passing when: Need to share open files, privilege separation, custom IPC design, preserve file state
Choose pipes for: Simple parent-child communication, stream-oriented data
Choose message queues for: Multiple producers/consumers, persistent messages
